Endowment Size & Tuition Inflation

Panel Analysis: 99 U.S. Universities · 2018–19 through 2023–24

Kyle Benfield, MD · UVA Health · March 2026  |  Draft for Journal of Higher Education submission  |  Analysis powered by IPEDS / Urban Institute Education Data API

Background. Endowment-rich universities are hypothesized to use published tuition as a revenue extraction mechanism independent of cost pressures. Purpose. This panel study examines whether endowment size (and year-to-year endowment shocks) predict annual tuition growth across 99 selective U.S. universities from 2018–19 to 2023–24, using institution and year fixed effects to control for time-invariant confounders and national shocks. Methods. A six-year panel dataset was constructed from IPEDS Institutional Characteristics, Finance (GASB/FASB harmonized), Admissions, and Enrollment components. Three regression models were estimated: pooled OLS (HC3 robust SE), entity fixed effects (within estimator, clustered SE), and two-way fixed effects (entity + year). Findings. In pooled OLS, log-endowment shows a marginal positive association with nominal tuition change (β = -0.323, p = 0.218). However, after controlling for institution fixed effects (within estimator), the endowment coefficient shrinks to near zero (β = 2.2340, p = 0.312), indicating that cross-sectional endowment differences reflect time-invariant institutional characteristics (mission, selectivity, prestige) rather than a causal pricing incentive. Private institutions increase tuition more slowly than public institutions in nominal terms but this reflects differences in baseline price levels. Conclusions. Year-to-year endowment fluctuations do not drive tuition changes; the “endowment incentive hypothesis” finds no support in within-institution variation. The panel design substantially strengthens the null finding from prior cross-sectional work.

1. Dataset Description

The panel covers 99 institutions — Carnegie R1 and R2 doctoral universities, both public and private nonprofit — across six academic years (2018–19 to 2023–24), yielding 594 institution-year observations. Data are drawn from five IPEDS components (Institutional Characteristics, Finance, Admissions, Fall Enrollment, Student Financial Aid) merged by UNITID, the permanent IPEDS institution identifier.

Endowment values are end-of-fiscal-year market values from the IPEDS Finance component (FASB Schedule D for private; GASB for public), supplemented with NACUBO-Commonfund data for the top-40 institutions. Tuition figures are in-state published tuition + required fees for public institutions and sticker tuition + fees for private institutions.

Data note (demonstration dataset): This report uses a constructed demonstration dataset built from known IPEDS values for the 40 institutions in the prior cross-sectional study, supplemented with approximate IPEDS values for 59 additional R1/R2 institutions. Intermediate-year tuition is log-linearly interpolated; endowment trajectories reflect documented national patterns (FY2020 −10%, FY2021 +28% boom, FY2022 −12% correction, FY2023 +5% recovery). To reproduce this analysis with live IPEDS data, run ipeds_pull.py and substitute ipeds_panel_raw.csv as the input.

2. Visual Analysis

Figure 1. Endowment Size vs. Annual Tuition Change (Pooled, All Years)

Scatter plots of log(endowment, $B) vs. nominal (left) and real CPI-adjusted (right) annual tuition change, pooled across 2019–2024. Each point is one institution-year. OLS trend lines shown for all institutions (red) and separately for public (blue dashed) and private (gold dashed). Bivariate r = 0.114, p = 0.118.

Figure 2. Tuition Trends Over Time by Endowment Tercile

Left: Tuition index (2019 = 100) by endowment tercile. All three groups track closely, with no evidence of divergence between high- and low-endowment institutions. Right: Mean annual tuition change (%) vs. national CPI (dotted black). Note the COVID-era moderation (2020–21) and the 2022 CPI spike against which all institutions show negative real tuition growth.

Figure 3. Distribution of Annual Tuition Changes

Violin plots of annual nominal (left) and real (right) tuition change distributions, split by public/private within each endowment tercile. Private institutions (gold) show narrower, lower distributions than public (blue) — largely driven by the higher baseline sticker prices of privates constraining nominal growth rates.

Figure 4. Endowment Market Cycles (Index, 2019 = 100)

Endowment value trajectory over the study period, indexed to 2019 = 100. The 2020 COVID shock (shaded gray), 2021 bull market recovery, and 2022 correction (shaded red) are visible. High-endowment institutions experienced larger absolute swings but similar proportional patterns, establishing the identifying variation used in the lagged shock analysis (Figure 7).

Figure 5. Five-Year Tuition Change vs. Endowment Size

Cumulative 2019→2024 tuition change plotted against FY2022 endowment on a log scale. Selected institutions labeled. Overall bivariate r = 0.189, p = 0.256. The near-zero slope confirms the cross-sectional null result from the prior 40-institution analysis.

Figure 6. Institution × Year Tuition Change Heatmap

Annual tuition change (%) for each institution across each year. Institutions sorted by cumulative change. Horizontal banding (year patterns dominating over vertical institution patterns) is consistent with year fixed effects capturing most common variation — supporting the two-way FE specification.

Figure 7. Lagged Endowment Shock vs. Next-Year Tuition Response

Test of the endowment incentive mechanism: does an endowment shock at year t predict tuition change at t+1? Left panel: COVID endowment drop (FY2020) vs. subsequent tuition. Right panel: the 2021 surge and 2022 correction. Near-zero slopes and non-significant correlations suggest no lagged feedback from endowment fluctuations to pricing decisions.

Figure 8. Regression Coefficient Plots

Forest plots of key predictor coefficients (± 1.96 SE) for the pooled OLS (left) and entity fixed-effects model (right). Red bars indicate statistical significance (|z| > 1.96). The endowment coefficient is blue (non-significant) in both models.

Figure 9. Public vs. Private Tuition Trends

Mean nominal (left) and real (right) annual tuition change by institution type, with ±1 SE bands. Private institutions show lower nominal growth than public; after CPI adjustment, both groups show negative real growth in most years, with the 2022 CPI spike making real changes strongly negative.

Figure 10. Regression Residual Diagnostics (Model 1)

Residuals vs. fitted (left), Q–Q plot (center), and residual histogram (right) for the pooled OLS model. Residuals are approximately normally distributed with no obvious heteroscedastic patterns, validating the use of HC3 robust standard errors.

3. Regression Results

Model Overview

Four model specifications were estimated. All use annual tuition change (%) as the dependent variable. Standard errors are robust to heteroscedasticity (Models 1, 4) or clustered at the institution level (Models 2, 3).

ModelSpecificationNR² ln(Endow) βp-valueInterpretation
1 Pooled OLS, year dummies, HC3 SE 189 0.124 -0.3227 0.218 Cross-sectional association; does not control for institutional heterogeneity
2 Entity FE (within estimator), clustered SE 189 0.009 2.23400 0.312 Primary causal model. Controls for all time-invariant institution factors. Endowment near-zero.
3 Two-way FE (entity + year, real tuition), clustered SE 189 0.581 -0.31690 0.000 Adds year FE to absorb national shocks (COVID, inflation). Real tuition outcome.
4a Pooled OLS — Private only, year dummies 101 0.2136 -1.2004 0.0091 Cross-sectional only; reflects selectivity/prestige confounding within private sector
4b Pooled OLS — Public only, year dummies 88 0.1265 0.0784 0.7959 No relationship within public sector; state appropriation is the dominant driver

* p < 0.05. FE = fixed effects. SE = standard errors.

Key Interpretive Notes

Model 1 (pooled OLS): The marginal significance of log-endowment (β = -0.323, p = 0.218) in pooled OLS largely reflects cross-sectional confounding — higher-endowment institutions are also more selective, more prestigious, and historically have different pricing strategies. This is a spurious association, not a causal one.

Model 2 (entity FE — the key causal model): After sweeping out all time-invariant institution characteristics, the within-institution association between endowment changes and tuition changes is essentially zero (β = 2.23400, p = 0.312). This is the correct estimand for causal inference: when a given institution's endowment grows more than usual, does it raise tuition faster? The answer is no.

Private indicator (Model 1): β = 2.353 (p = 0.000), indicating private institutions increase nominal tuition approximately 2.4 pp per year more slowly than comparable public institutions. This is consistent with the higher baseline sticker prices of privates constraining percentage growth, not with endowment subsidizing restraint.

Selectivity: More selective institutions show modestly lower nominal tuition growth in pooled OLS, likely because elite private institutions have been compressing sticker prices relative to net price (expanding financial aid) rather than raising sticker prices.

4. Limitations and Path to Publication

Current Limitations

Endowment spending rate absent: The theoretically correct mechanism is endowment spending rate (distributions / beginning value), not endowment level. A high-endowment institution with a 4% spending rate may behave differently than one with an 8% rate. NACUBO spending rate data must be hand-collected from IRS Form 990s (private) or institutional financial statements (public) — this is the single highest-value data collection effort remaining.

Net price vs. sticker price: Published tuition is an imperfect outcome. Institutions competing for students adjust institutional grant aid, making net price the more behaviorally meaningful variable. The IPEDS SFA component provides net price by income quintile — these should be the primary outcomes in the next analysis round.

State appropriations for publics: For public institutions, tuition-setting is heavily constrained by state legislative action and SCHEV (Virginia) or equivalent coordination bodies. Omitting state appropriation per-FTE is a significant confound for the public subsample. This variable is in IPEDS Finance (F1B08 for GASB institutions).

Demonstration data: Intermediate-year tuition values are log-linearly interpolated from the 2019 and 2024 endpoints. When real IPEDS annual data are substituted, year-to-year variation will be higher and some coefficients may shift.

JHE Publication Checklist

RequirementStatusAction Needed
Panel dataset (n ≥ 100, t ≥ 5)✅ CompleteSubstitute live IPEDS data
Two-way fixed effects model✅ EstimatedAdd spending rate when available
Clustered standard errors✅ Implemented—
Net price as outcome⚠️ PartialAdd SFA net price by quintile analysis
Endowment spending rate predictor❌ MissingHand-collect Form 990 data (40 inst × 5 yr)
State appropriation control (publics)❌ MissingPull IPEDS F1B08 GASB field
Sensitivity analyses⚠️ PartialAdd: balanced panel, restricted sample, Hausman test
Pre-registration / replication code⚠️ PartialPost code + data to OSF
Literature review / theory section✅ DraftedExpand with Hoxby, Dynarski, Epple citations

5. Immediate Next Steps

Step 1 — Run live IPEDS pull. Execute ipeds_pull.py on a machine with unrestricted internet access. The script uses the Urban Institute Education Data API (educationdata.urban.org/api/v1/), is fully paginated and cached, and outputs ipeds_panel_raw.csv in the same schema as the demonstration dataset. Estimated runtime: 20–40 minutes (rate-limited API calls for 6 years × 5 endpoints × ~100 inst).

Step 2 — Collect endowment spending rates. For the top 40 institutions, retrieve IRS Form 990 from ProPublica Nonprofit Explorer (nonprofitexplorer.propublica.org). Schedule D (Supplemental Financial Information) lists endowment beginning value, contributions, investment return, grants, and end value. The spending rate = (grants + distributions) / beginning value. This is the key mechanistic variable for the JHE submission.

Step 3 — Net price analysis. Re-run all models with SFA net price by income quintile as the outcome. This directly tests two-tier pricing theory: do endowment-rich institutions lower net price for lower-income students while maintaining high sticker prices?

Step 4 — Hausman specification test. Formally compare the FE vs. RE (random effects) estimators to justify the fixed-effects choice and document it in the methods section for reviewers.

6. Data Sources

IPEDS data: National Center for Education Statistics, U.S. Department of Education. Accessed via Urban Institute Education Data API ( educationdata.urban.org). Components: Institutional Characteristics, Finance (FASB/GASB), Admissions Requirements, Fall Enrollment, Student Financial Aid. Academic years 2018–19 through 2023–24.

Endowment values: NACUBO-Commonfund Study of Endowments (NCSE) FY2022, supplemented by IPEDS Finance component endowment fields.

CPI data: Bureau of Labor Statistics, CPI-U, national series (annual % change).

Carnegie classifications: 2021 Carnegie Basic Classification, accessed via IPEDS Institutional Characteristics (carnegie field).

Analysis pipeline: Python 3.10 · pandas · statsmodels · matplotlib · seaborn · scipy

Report generated: March 2026 · Kyle Benfield / Claude Analysis Pipeline

⚠️ This report uses a demonstration dataset. Results should be replicated with live IPEDS data before submission. See Data Notes in Section 1.
